1: Job's tenth speech opens with devastating sarcasm, meeting Bildad's crushing speech with ironic counter-praise.
2: Eziartāh lĕlōʾ-kōaḥ: 'What help you have given to the powerless!' — the sarcasm is biting: ʿāzar (to help) turned mock-praise. Hôšaʿtā zerôaʿ lōʾ-ʿōz: 'what strength you have brought to the feeble arm!' — Bildad has done absolutely nothing for Job. The speech that claims to describe God's might has brought no practical help to the suffering man before him.
3: Mah-yāʿaṣtā lĕlōʾ ḥāḵmāh: 'What counsel you have given to one with no wisdom!' — the irony continues: yāʿaṣ (to advise, counsel) applied to Bildad's useless speech. Wĕtûšiyāh lārōḇ hôḏaʿtā: 'how abundantly you have offered sound understanding!' — tûšiyāh (sound wisdom, practical effectiveness): the rarest, most prized wisdom term used in mock-praise.
4: ʾet-mî higgadtā millîm: 'Who are you addressing with these words?' — the challenge to Bildad's intended audience. Ûmî-nĕšāmāh yāṣĕʾāh mimmekā: 'whose spirit spoke through you?' — nĕšāmāh (breath, spirit): Bildad claimed divine inspiration (cf. Job 32:8); Job challenges the source.
5: Hārĕpāʾîm yĕḥôlālû mittaḥat mayim: 'The shades of the dead tremble under the waters' — rĕpāʾîm (the Rephaim/shades): the dead in Sheol (cf. Isaiah 14:9; Proverbs 2:18). The trembling of the underworld dead before God establishes that divine authority penetrates even the cosmic underworld. Wĕšōkĕnêhem (and those who dwell beneath them): the layered depths of the cosmological underworld.
6: ʿĀrôm šeʾôl negdô: 'Sheol is naked/exposed before him' — ʿārôm (naked, exposed): nothing in Sheol is hidden from God. ʾAḇaddôn ʾên kĕsûth: 'Abaddon has no covering' — ʾaḇaddôn (Abaddon, destruction — the place of final destruction, cf. Revelation 9:11): no concealment possible in the deepest pit. Both Sheol and Abaddon appear together as the twin death-realms (cf. Proverbs 15:11).
7: Nōṭeh ṣāpôn ʿal-tōhû: 'He stretches out the north over the void' — tōhû (formlessness, void): the same word as Genesis 1:2 (tōhû wābōhû). Job uses the creation-vocabulary to describe the divine cosmological act. Tōleh ʾereṣ ʿal-bĕlî māh: 'he hangs the earth on nothing' — the remarkable cosmological statement that the earth is suspended on nothing (bĕlî māh): no visible support. The OT's most explicit statement of the earth's suspension in space.
8: ṣōrer mayim beʿābāyw: 'He wraps the waters in his thick clouds' — the cloud-water storage system of the hydrological cycle. Wĕlōʾ-nibqaʿ ʿānān taḥtām: 'the cloud holds without bursting' — the marvel that clouds hold their water: an implicit acknowledgment of the divine engineering of weather.
9: Mĕʾaḥēz pĕnê-kissēʾ: 'He screens the face of his throne' — kissēʾ (throne): the divine royal seat. The cloud-screen conceals the divine throne (cf. the Sinai cloud, Exodus 24:16). Piśtēhû ʿālāyw ʿănānô: 'he spreads his cloud over it' — the veil of divine transcendence around the throne.
10: Ḥôg ḥāḡag ʿal-pĕnê-mayim: 'He has drawn a circle on the face of the waters' — ḥûg (circle, vault): the horizon-circle marking the boundary between waters (cf. Proverbs 8:27; Isaiah 40:22). ʿad-taktît ʾôr ʿim-ḥōšeḵ: 'the boundary between light and dark' — the creation-separation of Gen 1 expressed as a drawn circle.
11: ʿAmmûḏê šāmayim yĕrōpĕpû: 'The pillars of heaven tremble' — the cosmological pillars supporting the sky vault. Wĕyitmahhû miggĕʿārātô: 'they are stunned at his rebuke' — gĕʿārāh (rebuke, thunderous command): God's authoritative speech that shakes cosmic architecture. The same term used for the divine rebuke of chaos-waters (Psalms 104:7; Isaiah 50:2).
12: Bĕkōḥô rāgaʿ hayyām: 'By his power he calmed the sea' — rāgaʿ (to stir up or calm — the term is ambiguous; both meanings appear in the OT). ûḇiṯḇûnātô māḥaṣ Rāhaḇ: 'by his understanding he crushed Rahab' — Rāhaḇ: the sea-dragon/chaos monster (cf. Psalms 89:10; Isaiah 51:9), the mythological figure for the primordial waters of chaos. God's creation is the defeat of chaos.
13: Rûḥô šāmayim šiprāh: 'His Spirit made the heavens beautiful' — šāprāh (to be beautiful, clear): the Spirit's aesthetic dimension of creation. Ḥōlĕlāh yāḏô nāḥāš bārîaḥ: 'his hand transfixed the twisting serpent' — nāḥāš bārîaḥ (the twisting/fleeing serpent): the Leviathan/sea-serpent figure (cf. Isaiah 27:1; Revelation 12:9). Creation as divine defeat of chaos-serpent.
14: Hēn-ʾēlleh qĕṣôt dĕrākāyw: 'These things are but the edges of his ways' — qāṣeh (edge, extremity): what Job has described — cosmic creation, defeat of chaos, pillars of heaven — are merely the outermost fringes of divine activity. Wĕremes dābār nišmaʿ-bô: 'how faint a whisper we catch of him' — remes (a whisper, a murmur): the great theophanic acts are a barely audible whisper of the divine fullness. Rāʿam gĕḇûrātô mî yiṯbônān: 'the thunder of his power — who can understand?' — the doxological close. Job ends his survey of God's cosmic power with epistemological humility, not despair.
